patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious grocery assistant manager. Start with: “I’ve been feeling really tired and kind of short of breath lately.” Speak tangentially, jumping from work stress to symptoms. Volunteer: getting winded on stairs, headaches/brain fog, and your heart pounding with normal activity. If asked about other symptoms, add: dizziness on standing, looking pale, chest tightness when hurrying that stops with rest, brittle nails, and craving/chewing ice. Do NOT mention heavy periods, clots, pad changes, post-period fatigue, daily ibuprofen use, or stomach burning unless specifically asked about menstrual bleeding, pain meds, or stomach/black stools. Only admit (reluctantly) bright red blood on toilet paper if directly asked about rectal bleeding; act embarrassed. If asked about mood, cautiously share feeling down/overwhelmed and fear being dismissed as “just stress.” Misattribute symptoms to thyroid or anxiety; mention more coffee/energy drinks. Frequently ask about cost, time off work, and avoiding ER bills; doubt generics but worry brands are expensive. If clinician is empathetic, soften, provide more detail, and say you’re worried something serious is going on.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old daycare assistant, anxious but clear. Start with: “I can’t stop having diarrhea, and there’s blood in it.” Early in the visit, volunteer: diarrhea started 2 days ago, frequent, blood and mucus, crampy belly pain before you run to the bathroom, feeling feverish and wiped out. If asked, add: going every 30–60 minutes (sometimes little comes out), pain/tenesmus, nausea/low appetite, fever 101–102°F at home, dry mouth and peeing less, vomited twice day 1 only, no recent antibiotics. Do not mention unless specifically asked about exposures/meds/weight/dizziness: a few pounds lost, lightheaded on standing, taking ibuprofen, changed diapers at daycare this week. Resist disclosing unless asked directly and nonjudgmentally about sexual history or accidents: unprotected anal sex with a new partner 1 week ago; a couple incontinence accidents. Express misconceptions: think it’s food poisoning from takeout chicken; history of “sensitive stomach/IBS”; worried about Crohn’s because cousin has it. Economic concerns: fear missing work/losing pay; hope for a prescription without tests; worry you can’t afford stool testing and might ration meds. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears, and answer more openly.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Shigellosis (acute bacillary dysentery due to Shigella species)
required_evidence: Acute onset diarrhea, often frequent and small-volume

Signs of inflammatory diarrhea (systemic symptoms and/or leukocytosis/neutrophilia on CBC)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner if worsening); ensure symptom improvement within 48-72 hours of antibiotics and hydration

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious, tangential project manager. Start by saying: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s been going on for weeks.” Volunteer: long time to fall asleep, frequent awakenings, daytime exhaustion/irritability. Ramble about work stress and performance reviews.

If asked, describe racing thoughts at bedtime, unrefreshing sleep, early-morning waking, forgetfulness/trouble focusing, and heavy reliance on coffee/energy drinks. Say you tried melatonin with little benefit.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about habits/substances: scrolling on your phone in bed for an hour+, irregular weekend sleep schedule, evening alcohol to get sleepy then waking later, occasional decongestant/cold meds, low daylight/exercise.

Resist/feel embarrassed if asked about anxiety/panic, cannabis/THC gummies, and especially snoring/pauses in breathing; downplay at first, then admit if the clinician is nonjudgmental.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid/ADHD/“something serious” because of nighttime racing heart and morning headaches. Emphasize cost/time concerns; assume sleep studies/CBT-I are expensive and prefer OTC options.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Choline bitartrate 500 mg PO daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Melatonin 3 mg PO nightly as needed
confounders: High caffeine intake (3-4 cups coffee/day, last cup often after 3 PM)
Shift work/irregular sleep schedule (rotating early and late shifts)
SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline taken in the evening)
Possible restless legs syndrome symptoms (urge to move legs at night; low ferritin)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (snoring reported by partner; elevated BMI)
Perimenopausal symptoms (intermittent night sweats/hot flashes)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Chronic insomnia disorder
acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia disorder due to another medical condition (if labs/history support, e.g., hyperthyroidism, iron deficiency)
Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder (if history supports delayed sleep phase)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or <3 months for short-term)
Clinically significant daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood changes, concentration problems, reduced performance)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep (not explained by insufficient sleep opportunity)
Not better explained by another sleep disorder, substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of hyperthyroidism as a contributor)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal or mildly low; if low, consider iron deficiency contributing to sleep disturbance/restless legs symptoms
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be insufficient/low-normal; not diagnostic for insomnia

medications: Melatonin 0.5-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before desired bedtime (especially if circadian component suspected)
Doxepin 3-6 mg PO nightly for sleep maintenance insomnia (if needed after CBT-I)
non_pharmacological: CBT-I as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol close to bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, bedroom reserved for sleep/sex, cool/dark/quiet environment
If awake >20 minutes, leave bed and do a quiet activity until sleepy, then return to bed
Review and reduce contributing substances/medications (nicotine, alcohol, stimulants); assess timing/role of choline supplement and discontinue if it worsens arousal
Screen for obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome, depression/anxiety; treat or refer if suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess sleep diary outcomes, adherence to CBT-I strategies, medication response/side effects; sooner if worsening daytime impairment or safety concerns

must_include: Definition of insomnia disorder and how it is diagnosed (sleep complaint + daytime impairment + frequency/duration)
Acknowledge and rule out common medical contributors using history and available labs (thyroid dysfunction, anemia/iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, glucose abnormalities)
Set expectations: improvement is gradual; goal is better sleep efficiency and daytime function
CBT-I is first-line and more durable than medications
If medication is used: lowest effective dose, short-term/targeted use, review side effects (next-day sedation, dizziness, anticholinergic effects with doxepin though minimal at low dose)
How and when to take melatonin (timing relative to bedtime; avoid high doses)
Assess patient goals and worries (dependence, grogginess, safety at work/driving)
Discuss supplement use (choline): limited evidence for insomnia; consider stopping if activating; review all OTC products
Provide return precautions: severe daytime sleepiness, falls, confusion, worsening mood/suicidal thoughts, loud snoring/witnessed apneas
Sleep diary tracking (bedtime, wake time, awakenings, naps, caffeine/alcohol)
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine guidance and evening routine changes
Regular daytime exercise (avoid vigorous exercise close to bedtime) and morning light exposure if delayed sleep phase suspected

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit sleep complaint details (onset/maintenance/early awakening), duration/frequency, daytime impairment, schedule, sleep environment, naps, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine, exercise timing, screen use, shift work/jet lag; screen for red flags (suicidality, severe depression/mania, parasomnias, RLS symptoms, OSA symptoms), pregnancy/breastfeeding, and substance use.

Medication and supplement reconciliation including OTCs (antihistamines, decongestants, stimulants), and specifically choline use; prior insomnia treatments and response; assess patient goals and concerns (dependence, next-day grogginess, driving/work safety).
Order labs to assess common medical contributors when not recently available: TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron studies, vitamin B12, fasting glucose or HbA1c (or review if already done).
Review available lab results to support ruling out thyroid dysfunction, anemia/iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, and glucose abnormalities as contributors.

Make/confirm insomnia disorder diagnosis based on sleep complaint + daytime impairment + frequency/duration, and differentiate from circadian rhythm disorder, OSA, RLS, mood/anxiety disorders, medication/substance-induced insomnia.

Explain diagnosis and set expectations (gradual improvement; goals include sleep efficiency and daytime function). Provide CBT-I first-line plan and lifestyle/sleep hygiene education including sleep diary, stimulus control, sleep restriction principles, and guidance on caffeine/alcohol/nicotine, evening routine, exercise timing, and morning light if delayed sleep phase suspected.

If melatonin is considered, counsel on appropriate timing (typically 1–2 hours before desired bedtime for sleep onset issues; earlier for circadian delay), low doses (e.g., 0.5–3 mg), avoid high doses, and review possible side effects.
Required safety check prior to any prescription; verify allergies and prior adverse reactions to sedatives/hypnotics (e.g., doxepin, trazodone, zolpidem, benzodiazepines) and excipients.

Required safety check prior to prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds/supplements (CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids, antihistamines; CYP interactions for doxepin; serotonergic agents if considering trazodone).

Required safety check prior to prescribing; assess contraindications (untreated OSA, severe COPD, pregnancy considerations, history of complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, narrow-angle glaucoma/urinary retention for anticholinergic agents, bipolar/mania risk, severe hepatic impairment depending on agent).

If indicated after CBT-I discussion and safety checks, prescribe short-term/targeted pharmacotherapy at lowest effective dose (e.g., low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance) with counseling on next-day sedation, dizziness/falls risk, and minimal but possible anticholinergic effects at low dose; avoid long-term hypnotic reliance.
Arrange follow-up in 2–4 weeks to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, response/side effects if medication used, and reassess for OSA/RLS/mood contributors; provide return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety counseling (driving/work), and clear plan.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits insomnia diagnostic criteria explicitly: sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment, occurring at least 3 nights/week for at least 3 months (or clearly states when criteria are not met and labels as short-term insomnia).

Agent screens for and addresses red flags and common alternate diagnoses: OSA (snoring/witnessed apneas), RLS (urge to move legs), circadian delay/shift work, medication/substance-related insomnia, depression/anxiety/mania, and suicidality.

Agent reviews medical contributors using history and available labs, and orders/requests TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron studies, B12, and glucose testing when not available; interpretation is clinically appropriate.
Agent explains insomnia disorder in patient-friendly language and sets expectations that improvement is gradual, focusing on sleep efficiency and daytime function rather than perfect sleep.
Agent recommends CBT-I as first-line and describes core components (sleep diary, stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies) and why it is more durable than medications.

Agent provides lifestyle counseling: caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing, consistent wake time, limiting naps, wind-down routine, exercise guidance (avoid vigorous activity close to bedtime), and morning light exposure when delayed sleep phase is suspected.
Agent asks about patient goals and concerns (dependence, grogginess, safety at work/driving) and tailors the plan accordingly.
Agent addresses supplement use including choline: acknowledges limited evidence for insomnia, reviews all OTC products, and suggests stopping or adjusting if activating or interfering with sleep.
If melatonin is discussed, agent gives correct timing guidance relative to bedtime and discourages high doses; mentions possible side effects and variability in OTC product quality.

If any prescription is offered, agent uses lowest effective dose, recommends short-term/targeted use, and reviews common adverse effects (next-day sedation, dizziness/falls; anticholinergic effects with doxepin are possible though minimal at low dose).
Before prescribing, agent performs all required critical safety checks: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication assessment; failure of any is a critical error.

Agent provides clear return precautions: severe daytime sleepiness, falls, confusion, worsening mood or suicidal thoughts, and loud snoring/witnessed apneas; advises avoiding driving/operating machinery if sedated.

Agent schedules appropriate follow-up (typically 2–4 weeks) and specifies what will be reviewed (sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, medication effects/side effects, reconsideration of alternative diagnoses).

minimal_paths.description: Meets criteria for insomnia disorder; no urgent comorbidity identified; management with CBT-I, lifestyle changes, sleep diary, optional low-dose melatonin education; no prescription needed.
must_collect: exercise timing and evening routine/screen use
current meds and OTC/supplements including choline
steps: ASK (sleep complaint + impairment + frequency/duration + schedule + red flags)
ORDER_LAB (TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron, B12, glucose/HbA1c if not recent)
DIAGNOSE (insomnia disorder; rule out common contributors)
EDUCATE (diagnosis explanation + CBT-I + sleep diary + lifestyle)

minimal_paths.description: Insomnia disorder with significant distress/impairment despite initial behavioral efforts; after ruling out key contributors and completing safety checks, short-term medication is added alongside CBT-I.
must_collect: prior CBT-I/sleep hygiene attempts and response
snoring/witnessed apneas and severe daytime sleepiness
complete medication list including OTC/supplements and alcohol use
contraindication history (complex sleep behaviors, glaucoma/urinary retention, respiratory disease, pregnancy/breastfeeding)
steps: ASK (sleep complaint + impairment + frequency/duration + red flags)
ASK (meds/OTC/supplements including choline; goals/concerns; prior treatments)
ORDER_LAB (TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron, B12, glucose/HbA1c if not recent)

PRESCRIBE (short-term lowest effective dose; counsel side effects and safety)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (2–4 weeks; reassess and deprescribe plan)

acceptable_variants.description: If recent labs already available in the chart, agent may skip ORDER_LAB and proceed directly to GET_RESULTS and interpretation, provided medical contributors are still addressed explicitly.

If circadian delay is suspected, agent emphasizes morning light exposure and earlier melatonin timing, and may diagnose delayed sleep-wake phase disorder with comorbid insomnia; still provides CBT-I elements and follow-up.

If OSA risk is high (loud snoring/witnessed apneas, significant daytime sleepiness), agent avoids sedative-hypnotic prescribing, provides safety counseling, and prioritizes referral/diagnostic evaluation while still addressing insomnia with CBT-I.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 44-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I keep getting this burning when I pee, and I feel like I have to go all the time.” Volunteer: burning with urination, frequent trips with only a little urine, and feeling like you still need to pee right after. If asked, add: stronger-smelling and cloudy urine, low belly pressure/discomfort, low-grade fever at home, mild nausea without vomiting, no new vaginal discharge, and no sores/bumps. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about habits: wiping back-to-front sometimes, holding urine for long stretches at work, low water intake, and using a new scented bubble bath/soap. Withhold sexual history unless asked directly; if asked, admit a new partner and inconsistent condom use and say you were worried it might be an STI and felt embarrassed. Express misconceptions: you thought it might be a yeast infection due to irritation, tried lots of cranberry juice, and wondered if stress/lack of sleep caused it. If the clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and become more open. Ask for lowest-cost tests first, prefer generics, and want a plan minimizing time off work.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute bacterial cystitis (uncomplicated urinary tract infection)
required_evidence: Dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, suprapubic discomfort without vaginal discharge/irritation
Afebrile or low-grade temperature and no systemic toxicity
No flank pain or costovertebral angle tenderness (argues against pyelonephritis)
Basic labs not suggestive of severe infection or sepsis (no marked leukocytosis; stable platelets/hemoglobin)
Normal or near-normal BUN and electrolytes suggesting no significant dehydration/AKI

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (often normal in uncomplicated cystitis)

medications: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg PO twice daily for 5 days
Phenazopyridine 200 mg PO three times daily after meals for up to 2 days (optional for dysuria)
non_pharmacological: Encourage oral hydration as tolerated
Avoid bladder irritants (caffeine, alcohol) until symptoms improve
Return precautions: fever, flank pain, vomiting, worsening symptoms, or no improvement within 48–72 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48–72 hours if not improving; sooner if red-flag symptoms develop

must_include: Likely bladder infection (lower UTI) based on symptoms and absence of kidney infection signs
Explain difference between cystitis vs pyelonephritis and what symptoms would suggest escalation
Antibiotic choice and duration (nitrofurantoin 5 days) and importance of completing the course
Expected timeline for symptom improvement (often within 24–48 hours)
If using phenazopyridine: symptomatic only, limit to 2 days, may turn urine orange
Review penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassure that nitrofurantoin is not a penicillin/cephalosporin
Discuss common side effects and when to stop/seek care (e.g., rash, breathing difficulty, severe GI symptoms)
Hydration and avoiding bladder irritants
Prevention counseling: urinate after sex, avoid spermicides if recurrent, wipe front-to-back

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for dysuria/UTI (symptoms, duration, pregnancy risk, red flags for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI, prior UTIs/antibiotics, STI risk, comorbidities).
Order urinalysis +/- urine culture per local practice; consider pregnancy test if pregnancy possible; labs support diagnosis and guide therapy if atypical/recurrent.
Review UA/culture (if available) to support cystitis and identify need for escalation or alternative diagnosis.
Diagnose likely acute uncomplicated cystitis vs alternative/complicated infection; explicitly assess for absence/presence of pyelonephritis signs.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergy history and reaction details (e.g., penicillin urticarial rash) and ensure chosen antibiotic is safe.
Non-critical safety check: review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., anticoagulants, other antibiotics) and counsel accordingly.
Initiate first-line therapy for uncomplicated cystitis when appropriate: nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x 5 days (if no contraindications) +/- short-course phenazopyridine for symptom relief.
Provide diagnosis explanation, treatment expectations, side effects/return precautions, and prevention/lifestyle counseling.
Set follow-up plan: reassess if not improving in 48 hours, sooner if worsening; advise urgent care for pyelonephritis/red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and safety-netting.

required_evidence: Chronic, fatigable muscle weakness beginning in infancy/childhood (ptosis, ophthalmoparesis, bulbar symptoms, limb-girdle weakness) with fluctuation and worsened by exertion/illness

No autoimmune myasthenia gravis features (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK antibodies if tested; lack of thymoma history)

Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS (e.g., biallelic pathogenic variants in GMPPB/DPAGT1/ALG genes) or a strong phenotype consistent with glycosylation-defect CMS while awaiting results

Baseline and periodic assessment of respiratory function (e.g., FVC/NIF if available) and swallowing safety; low threshold for urgent evaluation if dyspnea, weak cough, or aspiration symptoms
Physical therapy with energy conservation strategies; avoid overexertion, ensure adequate rest, treat intercurrent infections early

medications: levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily
clobazam 10 mg nightly
lisinopril 10 mg once daily
acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever
confounders: recent pharyngitis 2-3 weeks ago with incomplete antibiotic course (suggests post-streptococcal etiology but also raises concern for persistent infection)
ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) can worsen hyperkalemia and complicate renal function interpretation during acute kidney injury
limited symptom reporting and variable exam cooperation due to developmental disability, potentially delaying recognition of edema, oliguria, or systemic symptoms
intermittent NSAID use for headaches reported by caregiver (can cause hemodynamic AKI and mimic/worsen nephritic presentation)
trace leukocyte esterase and mild pyuria could be misattributed to UTI despite negative nitrites and presence of RBC casts

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced nephritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury (intrinsic renal) due to acute interstitial nephritis
Acute tubular necrosis (if ischemic/toxic exposure and muddy casts support)
required_evidence: Acute or subacute rise in serum creatinine with reduced eGFR consistent with intrinsic AKI
Clinical context suggestive of nephritis/AIN (e.g., recent new medication exposure such as antibiotics/NSAIDs/PPIs, +/- fever, rash, arthralgias)
Electrolyte/acid-base pattern compatible with AKI (e.g., hyperkalemia and/or metabolic acidosis)
Exclusion of pre-renal dehydration and post-renal obstruction by history/exam and appropriate evaluation (e.g., volume status assessment; consider renal ultrasound if concern for obstruction)

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated above baseline (acute rise consistent with AKI)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated, typically with BUN:creatinine ratio not markedly >20:1 (suggesting intrinsic rather than purely pre-renal)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be mildly low depending on volume status
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal; may be elevated if non-anion gap metabolic acidosis present
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Often low (metabolic acidosis) in moderate/severe AKI
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Often normal; may be low in significant AKI

medications: Stop suspected offending drug(s) immediately (e.g., NSAID/PPI/antibiotic if recently started) PO/IV once
Normal saline 0.9% IV bolus 500-1000 mL once, then reassess volume status and urine output (only if hypovolemic; avoid if volume overloaded)
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO three times daily for up to 48 hours (if hyperkalemia without ECG instability)
Furosemide 20-40 mg IV once (if hyperkalemia with volume overload and adequate perfusion; not as sole therapy for severe hyperkalemia)

Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg/day) for 1-2 weeks then taper over 2-6 weeks (consider if AIN strongly suspected/confirmed and kidney function not improving within 3-5 days after stopping offending agent, or if severe AKI; ensure infection excluded)
non_pharmacological: Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, IV contrast if possible, aminoglycosides) and renally dose all medications
Assess volume status; strict intake/output monitoring; daily weights
Low potassium diet if hyperkalemic; moderate protein intake during AKI as clinically appropriate
Early nephrology consultation if creatinine rising rapidly, severe electrolyte/acid-base disturbance, oliguria/anuria, or concern for biopsy/dialysis
Consider renal ultrasound if any concern for obstruction or unclear etiology of AKI
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat BMP (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarbonate) within 24-48 hours; sooner (within 4-6 hours) if hyperkalemia or acidosis is present or worsening. Outpatient follow-up within 1 week after discharge with repeat labs if improving.

must_include: Kidney inflammation causing reduced filtration (acute kidney injury pattern)
Likely trigger is a medication-related immune reaction (acute interstitial nephritis) when supported by history
Need to stop the suspected offending medication and monitor kidney function closely
Warning signs requiring urgent care (reduced urine output, weakness/palpitations, shortness of breath, confusion)
Immediate discontinuation of suspected causative drug(s)
Supportive care: fluids only if dehydrated; avoid excess fluids if swollen/short of breath
Plan to manage dangerous complications (high potassium, acidosis) and when dialysis may be needed
If steroids are used: rationale (reduce inflammation), expected duration, and key risks (infection, hyperglycemia, mood changes, GI upset)
Penicillin allergy should be clearly documented; avoid penicillins and assess cross-reactivity risk if considering cephalosporins
Medication review for nephrotoxins and interactions; confirm no OTC NSAID use
Prognosis: many improve after removing trigger, but recovery may take weeks; possibility of incomplete recovery if severe/delayed
Avoid NSAIDs and unreviewed supplements; use acetaminophen within recommended limits for pain/fever unless contraindicated
Diet guidance if potassium is elevated (limit high-potassium foods) and hydration guidance tailored to volume status
Importance of follow-up labs and keeping a list of medications and allergies

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for nephritis/AKI pattern and acute interstitial nephritis (AIN): onset/timeline, urine changes, systemic symptoms (fever, rash, arthralgias), recent infections, new meds (esp. antibiotics, PPIs, NSAIDs), OTC/supplements, volume status (vomiting/diarrhea, poor intake), and red flags (oliguria, dyspnea, chest pain, confusion).

Order initial AKI/nephritis workup to confirm kidney inflammation/impairment and identify dangerous complications: BMP/CMP (Cr, BUN, K, HCO3), CBC with differential (eosinophilia), urinalysis with microscopy (protein, blood, WBCs, casts), urine protein/creatinine ratio, urine eosinophils if available, urine sodium/FeNa if indicated, CK if rhabdo concern; consider complements/ANA/ANCA/anti-GBM if glomerulonephritis suspected; renal ultrasound if obstruction risk.

Interpret results to classify AKI and differentiate AIN vs glomerulonephritis vs pre/post-renal causes; identify emergent abnormalities (hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, rapidly rising creatinine, pulmonary edema).
Make working diagnosis (e.g., medication-related AIN causing nephritis/AKI) supported by history/labs; document differential and severity; decide need for urgent escalation/nephrology referral.
Before any prescribing, confirm and document allergies (explicitly penicillin allergy), reaction type/severity, and timing to guide safe medication selection.
Before prescribing, review current meds/OTC/supplements for nephrotoxins and interactions (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, diuretics, trimethoprim, spironolactone, lithium, metformin, contrast exposure).

Perform contraindication screen prior to ordering or prescribing: pregnancy status when relevant, baseline CKD, volume status (dehydration vs overload), active infection risk if considering steroids, uncontrolled diabetes, GI bleed risk, and signs requiring ED referral.

If not already included or if severity warrants, order repeat/confirmatory tests for contraindication/complication checks before treatment decisions: repeat potassium/ECG if hyperkalemia suspected, VBG/ABG if acidosis suspected, repeat creatinine trend, and infectious evaluation if febrile (urine culture/blood cultures as indicated).
Confirm safety-critical contraindications/complications (e.g., severe hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload) that alter management and indicate urgent care/dialysis consideration.

Implement treatment plan consistent with safety checks: discontinue suspected offending drug(s); avoid nephrotoxins; prescribe supportive measures (e.g., antiemetic if needed, renal-dose adjustments) and consider steroids only when appropriate after infection exclusion and with risk counseling; avoid penicillins and carefully assess cephalosporin cross-reactivity if considered.

Communicate diagnosis, treatment, prognosis, red flags, medication avoidance (NSAIDs), diet/hydration guidance tailored to volume status and potassium level, and importance of follow-up labs/med list/allergy list.

Arrange timely follow-up and monitoring: repeat BMP/urinalysis within 24–72h depending on severity; nephrology referral if significant AKI, unclear etiology, heavy proteinuria/hematuria, or poor improvement; ensure escalation plan for worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering concerns, and ensuring safety net instructions and follow-up are in place.

Feeling short of breath doing normal things like walking around the house
Low-grade fever and chills
Feeling unusually tired
if_asked: Tight feeling in the chest when I take a deep breath
Wheezing or a whistling sound sometimes when breathing out
Night sweats

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when the clinician asks about results of any recent ER visit, bronchoscopy, or lung tests

general_concerns: Thinks the cough is just a lingering viral bronchitis and that antibiotics would “knock it out.”
Believes eosinophilic lung problems only happen to people with severe asthma or “bad allergies,” so he doubts it fits him.
Assumes any abnormal cells in lung fluid mean cancer, and worries the bronchoalveolar findings are proof of a tumor.
Thinks a normal chest X-ray in the past means his lungs can’t have a serious inflammatory condition now.
Believes inhaling dust at work is the only possible cause and is unaware medications, vaping/smoking, or new environmental exposures can trigger lung inflammation.
economic_concerns: Worries that additional imaging and bronchoscopy-related follow-up will be too expensive even with insurance and asks if tests can be minimized.
Concerned about missing work due to appointments and potential treatment side effects, especially because he works night shifts and has limited paid time off.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male warehouse logistics supervisor on night shifts. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had this cough that won’t go away, and I’m getting more short of breath.” Volunteer: dry cough ~1 week worsening, short of breath with normal activity (walking around the house), low-grade fever/chills, unusual fatigue. If asked, add: chest tightness with deep breaths, occasional wheeze/whistling on exhale, night sweats, can’t lie flat without more breathlessness, and once or twice small rusty/pink-tinged sputum. Do not volunteer that you started a new THC vape cartridge; if directly asked about vaping/drugs, you minimize: “Just occasional vaping, nothing major,” and you prefer it not documented. Only reveal it if the clinician explains why it matters and assures confidentiality. If asked about exposures/meds/weight: mention new medication started 2 weeks ago, possible dust/mold/cleaning spray exposure, unintentional weight loss over a month, and worse symptoms at night/early morning. Express misconceptions: you thought allergies/bronchitis/asthma. If met with empathy, you acknowledge and open up. Ask to minimize costly tests and visits due to insurance and missing work.

medications: Albuterol HFA 90 mcg 2 puffs inhaled q4-6h PRN wheeze
Fluticasone nasal spray 50 mcg 2 sprays each nostril daily
confounders: Recent initiation of cigarette smoking/vaping within past 3 weeks (can trigger acute eosinophilic pneumonia)
Recent course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with persistent symptoms (raises concern for infection and drug reaction)
Hemosiderin-laden macrophages suggesting possible diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (can mimic/overlap with vasculitis or Goodpasture syndrome despite negative ANCA/normal urinalysis)
Mild asthma with intermittent wheeze (can suggest asthma exacerbation or allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis in differential)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Eosinophilic pneumonia (acute or chronic; steroid-responsive eosinophilic lung disease)
acceptable_alternatives: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) with pulmonary involvement
required_evidence: Respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough) consistent with eosinophilic lung disease
Elevated absolute eosinophil count and/or elevated eosinophil percentage on CBC (may be absent early in acute eosinophilic pneumonia)
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP and/or ESR) supporting active inflammation

Supporting bronchoalveolar lavage findings consistent with inflammatory alveolitis; eosinophilia on BAL would support eosinophilic pneumonia (note: hemosiderin-laden macrophages also raise concern for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and should prompt assessment for hemoptysis/anemia and vasculitis)

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis) in acute presentations; may be normal or mildly elevated in chronic disease
expected_lab_results.Absolute eosinophil count: Elevated (commonly >500/µL; may be markedly elevated in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia; can be normal early in acute eosinophilic pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be decreased if concurrent alveolar hemorrhage suggested by hemosiderin-laden macrophages
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may be mildly elevated as an acute-phase reactant

medications: Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day PO once daily (typical adult 40 mg PO daily) for 2-4 weeks, then taper over 4-8 weeks based on symptoms and eosinophil count
If severe hypoxemia/respiratory failure: Methylprednisolone 60-125 mg IV every 6-8 hours initially, then transition to oral prednisone when improving
non_pharmacological: Assess oxygenation and provide supplemental oxygen as needed; consider hospital admission if hypoxemic, tachypneic, or clinically unstable
Review and discontinue any potential offending drugs or exposures if suspected (drug-induced eosinophilic pneumonia)
Evaluate for alternative causes if red flags present (e.g., diffuse alveolar hemorrhage/vasculitis suggested by hemosiderin-laden macrophages, falling hemoglobin, hemoptysis)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48-72 hours after starting systemic corticosteroids (earlier if severe), then again at 2-4 weeks with repeat CBC (including eosinophils) and symptom review; taper guided by clinical response and eosinophil normalization

must_include: The working diagnosis is eosinophilic pneumonia—an inflammatory lung condition involving eosinophils
Explain that labs show an eosinophilic/inflammatory pattern and that response to steroids is expected
Acknowledge that hemosiderin-laden macrophages can also suggest prior bleeding in the lungs and that the team will watch for hemoptysis or anemia and consider additional evaluation if needed
Systemic corticosteroids are first-line and usually lead to rapid improvement
How to take prednisone (daily dosing) and the importance of not stopping abruptly; taper plan depends on response
Key side effects to monitor (mood changes, insomnia, increased appetite, hyperglycemia, infection risk) and when to seek care
Confirm penicillin allergy history and reassure that the planned therapy is not a penicillin antibiotic
Discuss warning symptoms requiring urgent evaluation (worsening shortness of breath, new fever, chest pain, coughing blood, fainting)
Avoid suspected triggers/exposures (new medications, inhalational exposures) until reviewed
If on prolonged steroids: discuss infection precautions and follow-up monitoring

required_evidence: Recurrent migraine attacks with aura including fully reversible motor weakness (hemiparesis) and at least one other reversible aura symptom (visual/sensory/speech)

acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome (DOCK8-related combined immunodeficiency)

required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE for age (often >2000 IU/mL)

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (often in the thousands IU/mL)
expected_lab_results.WBC: May be elevated during infection; can be normal between infections
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Often normal; may be elevated with acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly low (anemia of chronic inflammation if frequent infections)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may be elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) during inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal at baseline; elevated during acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal at baseline; elevated during active inflammation/infection

correct_diagnosis.primary: Postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to acetylcholine receptor (AChR) deficiency (postsynaptic CMS)
Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to DOK7 mutation (postsynaptic CMS subtype)
required_evidence: Chronic (often childhood-onset) fatigable weakness with fluctuating severity (e.g., ptosis/ophthalmoparesis, bulbar symptoms, proximal limb weakness) without sensory loss
Symptoms worsened by exertion and improved by rest
No autoimmune features typical of acquired myasthenia gravis (e.g., negative history for thymoma/other autoimmune disease; absence of clear adult-onset pattern)
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (decrement on repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Genetic confirmation of a postsynaptic CMS variant when available (or strong phenotype consistent with postsynaptic CMS)

expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Potassium: Typically normal (monitor for hypokalemia if beta-2 agonist therapy is used)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30-60 mg PO every 4-6 hours while awake (titrate to benefit and tolerability; consider extended-release at bedtime if nocturnal/early morning symptoms)

Albuterol (salbutamol) 2-4 mg PO twice daily (or equivalent long-acting beta-2 agonist regimen) for selected postsynaptic CMS subtypes with inadequate response to pyridostigmine; monitor potassium and heart rate
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that can worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, magnesium-containing products, certain beta-blockers) unless clearly necessary and closely monitored
Energy conservation strategies and pacing; schedule demanding activities during best time of day
Referral to neuromuscular specialist and genetic counseling; confirm subtype to tailor therapy (some subtypes may worsen with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors)
Respiratory monitoring and action plan for bulbar/respiratory weakness (seek urgent care for dyspnea, weak cough, aspiration, rapidly worsening weakness)
Physical therapy focused on low-to-moderate intensity, rest breaks, and avoiding overexertion
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptom control and adverse effects in 2-4 weeks after starting or adjusting therapy; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if bulbar/respiratory symptoms or significant side effects occur

must_include: Condition is an inherited disorder of neuromuscular transmission (not autoimmune myasthenia gravis)
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with exertion; rest improves
Subtype matters because treatments differ and some medicines can worsen certain CMS types
Discuss warning signs that require urgent evaluation (breathing/swallowing problems)
Goal is to improve signal transmission at the neuromuscular junction and reduce fatigable weakness
How to take pyridostigmine (timing, expected onset/offset) and common side effects (cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions); when to call for help
If using beta-2 agonist therapy: rationale, expected timeframe for benefit, and monitoring for palpitations/tremor and low potassium
Emphasize avoiding medications/supplements that can worsen weakness (including magnesium-containing products) unless approved

Discuss family planning/genetic implications and offer genetic counseling
Set expectations: chronic condition with variable response; need for titration and follow-up
Activity pacing, planned rest, and avoiding overexertion that triggers worsening

Safety with swallowing (small bites, upright posture) if bulbar symptoms present; seek evaluation for aspiration risk
When to seek emergency care: shortness of breath, inability to swallow saliva, rapidly progressive weakness

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 14-year-old anxious but clear-speaking 8th grader who helps with childcare after school. Start with: “I get tired and weak really fast, and by the end of the day my eyelids droop.” Volunteer: weakness that worsens with repeated activity, droopy eyelids later in day, stairs/groceries wipe you out, voice fades with long talking. Resist bringing up unless directly asked in a private, nonjudgmental way: urinary urgency/leaks; sexual difficulty/fatigue; fear others think you’re lazy/faking. Offer misconceptions: think it’s anxiety, low iron, maybe MS; tried gluten-free without benefit. Ask about costs early; assume genetic tests/specialists/new meds are unaffordable. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, admit embarrassment, and share more details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS), glycosylation defect subtype
Congenital disorder of glycosylation with neuromuscular junction involvement (CMS phenotype)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset or lifelong fatigable weakness (ptosis, ophthalmoparesis, bulbar symptoms, proximal limb weakness) with fluctuation and worsening with exertion/illness
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
No acetylcholine receptor (AChR) or MuSK autoantibodies (to support non-autoimmune etiology) OR clinical course inconsistent with autoimmune MG
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-related CMS gene (e.g., GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1) OR strong suspicion based on phenotype and response pattern
Clinical response to cholinesterase inhibition (often partial) and/or 3,4-diaminopyridine; avoidance of immunosuppression as primary therapy unless clear autoimmune overlap

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from CMS itself)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (may be elevated only if intercurrent infection triggering worsening)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal (important baseline prior to agents that may affect cardiac conduction/arrhythmia risk)
expected_lab_results.CMP - CO2 (Bicarbonate): Often normal; may be low if acute respiratory compromise with metabolic acidosis or compensatory changes (not expected in stable patient)
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal (assess renal function for medication safety and hydration status)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 4-6 hours while awake; titrate to effect and tolerability (max individualized; monitor for cholinergic side effects)
Amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine) 10 mg PO three times daily; titrate cautiously based on response and adverse effects (do not exceed 80 mg/day total; consider ECG monitoring if risk factors)

If inadequate response or specific genotype/phenotype suggests benefit: Salbutamol (albuterol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or equivalent dosing per age/weight if pediatric); titrate and monitor heart rate and tremor
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, beta-blockers, certain antiarrhythmics, and sedatives/opioids when possible)
Vaccination and infection prevention; prompt treatment of respiratory infections to reduce exacerbations
Energy conservation, paced activity, and physical therapy focusing on low-impact strengthening without overfatigue
Swallow evaluation and dietary texture modification if bulbar symptoms/aspiration risk; consider speech therapy
Respiratory assessment if symptoms suggest hypoventilation (e.g., morning headaches, orthopnea); consider pulmonary function testing and nocturnal ventilation support if indicated
Genetic counseling for patient/family and referral to neuromuscular specialist
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular clinic follow-up in 2-4 weeks after medication initiation/titration (earlier within 48-72 hours if worsening bulbar/respiratory symptoms), then every 3-6 months once stable

must_include: This is a genetic (inborn) problem of neuromuscular junction signaling, not an autoimmune condition
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with exertion, illness, and certain medications
The glycosylation-related subtype affects proteins needed for effective neuromuscular transmission
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigability; response may be partial and requires dose titration
How to take pyridostigmine (timing with activities/meals) and common side effects (cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions) and when to seek help
Amifampridine risks (tingling, seizures at high doses, possible cardiac effects) and importance of not exceeding prescribed dose
Plan for monitoring response and adjusting therapy; genetic confirmation guides long-term management
Review allergy history and explicitly avoid azithromycin/macrolides due to prior worsening weakness/shortness of breath
Discuss red flags requiring urgent care: increasing shortness of breath, trouble swallowing, inability to handle secretions, rapidly worsening weakness
Clarify that immunosuppressants used for autoimmune myasthenia are not first-line for CMS unless there is separate evidence of autoimmunity
Medication avoidance list (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium-containing products, beta-blockers) and to inform all clinicians/pharmacists
Infection prevention and early evaluation for respiratory infections
Activity pacing, sleep optimization, and planning demanding tasks when medication effect is strongest

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF), autosomal dominant
acceptable_alternatives: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) flare (autoinflammatory periodic fever syndrome)
Hereditary periodic fever syndrome consistent with FMF (pending genetic confirmation)
required_evidence: Recurrent self-limited inflammatory attacks (fever with serositis such as peritonitis/pleuritis/pericarditis, or acute monoarthritis, or erysipelas-like erythema)
Elevated acute-phase reactants during attacks (CRP/ESR and/or Serum amyloid A, fibrinogen) with improvement between attacks
No evidence of bacterial infection (e.g., low/normal procalcitonin and no focal infectious source) and no alternative diagnosis explaining episodes
Personal/family history compatible with FMF and/or pathogenic MEFV variant (not required for initial treatment but supports diagnosis)
Assessment of risk for AA amyloidosis (persistently elevated SAA or proteinuria if available)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Mild to moderate leukocytosis with neutrophilia during an acute attack; may normalize between attacks
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may show mild anemia of inflammation if attacks are frequent/chronic
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) during inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during attack; decreases with resolution/colchicine control
expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated during attack; slower to normalize than CRP
expected_lab_results.Serum amyloid A (SAA): Elevated during attack; may remain elevated between attacks if disease is uncontrolled (key marker for amyloidosis risk)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Typically low/normal in non-infectious FMF flare; if markedly elevated, consider bacterial infection

medications: Colchicine 0.6 mg PO twice daily (adjust to 0.6 mg PO daily if intolerance; titrate to control and tolerability)
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (if no NSAID contraindications)
non_pharmacological: Hydration and rest during attacks; return precautions for severe/worsening pain, dyspnea, persistent high fever, or new focal infection symptoms
Adherence counseling: daily colchicine to prevent attacks and AA amyloidosis (not only during flares)
Monitor disease activity with symptoms plus CRP/SAA; consider referral to rheumatology/clinical genetics for confirmation and long-term management
If colchicine-resistant or intolerant: plan escalation to IL-1 inhibition (e.g., anakinra/canakinumab) under specialist supervision
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks after starting/adjusting colchicine, then every 3-6 months with symptom review and CRP/SAA monitoring

minimal_paths.description: Standard FMF evaluation with safe initiation/continuation of daily colchicine prophylaxis and monitoring plan.

steps: ASK: characterize attacks, red flags, meds/allergies, comorbid renal/hepatic disease, pregnancy status, infection likelihood, family history

GET_RESULTS: review inflammation markers and organ function; assess amyloidosis screening (proteinuria) if present

CHECK_INTERACTION: review for strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors and myotoxic co-meds; plan avoidance/adjustment
PRESCRIBE: daily colchicine with dose considerations and side-effect plan; avoid antibiotics unless infection evidence
EDUCATE: deliver all required communication milestones (diagnosis, treatment, concerns, lifestyle/monitoring, urgent-care triggers)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: timeframe to reassess symptoms and repeat labs; reinforce adherence and diary

minimal_paths.description: FMF flare suspected but includes explicit rule-out of bacterial infection before declining antibiotics; then safe colchicine plan.
steps: ASK: assess for infectious source and sepsis red flags alongside FMF flare features
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP, CRP, SAA (if available), urinalysis; add procalcitonin/cultures only if infection suspected
GET_RESULTS: interpret for inflammatory flare vs infection likelihood
DIAGNOSE: FMF flare/ongoing FMF with low likelihood of bacterial infection (if supported)
PRESCRIBE: daily colchicine (initiate/continue) with monitoring and interaction avoidance; no antibiotics without evidence
EDUCATE: explain why antibiotics not indicated; provide urgent-care thresholds and monitoring plan

acceptable_variants.description: Performs contraindication screening primarily through ASK (renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy, interacting drugs) and documents it as part of prescribing decision, while still completing explicit allergy and interaction tool checks before prescribing.

If already on colchicine, agent focuses on adherence, side effects, interaction review, and monitoring rather than initiating therapy, but still completes safety checks before any dose change/refill recommendation.

Orders SAA as optional/if available and otherwise uses CRP plus urinalysis/proteinuria as amyloidosis surveillance; still explicitly mentions SAA as preferred monitoring where available to satisfy communication milestone.
